insight: Endotherm Kit tends to be good in high-economy sets for its burst value, especially if there is a large absorber like Centurion that can be denied with freezes. Popping frostbites to kill Rhinos is generally not worth it; holding 1-charge Rhinos is preferable for defending Kit.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer posted a long set of unit-by-unit strategic notes in response to questions from shadourow.

insight: In base+Antima Comet, P2's opening DD DDE DDDC BAD is slightly too fast, so P1's DD DDE DDD DDDC BADD wins. When good soak (e.g., Plexo Cell) is available, a slower Antima is better, and you should not fear waiting until you can buy out Engineers over two turns.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer's unit-by-unit strategic notes discussing Antima Comet timing lines.

insight: Chrono Filter likely makes Antima Comet faster; whether to take a 3rd Engineer in Chrono+Antima sets depends on the specific set and available soak. Galvani Drone combined with Antima Comet is not an automatic win despite community perception.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer's unit notes on Antima Comet, expressing skepticism about the 'galvantima autowin' claim.

insight: When using Centrifuge, going for it on turn 2 is justified for Vai Mauronax but not for high-cost red/blue units like Odin or Luci, because the new Centrifuge does not provide enough gold to avoid awkward floating. If the opponent rushes those expensive units too fast, getting a Wall and building your own second while spending most of the tech is often better.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer's unit-by-unit notes discussing Centrifuge economy and tech-spending considerations.

insight: P1 Savior beats P2 BSO (Blastforge-Steelsplitter opener), so Savior is viable even with Walls vs Tarsiers if defense is carefully calculated in the early turns. P2 T3 Savior is especially vulnerable to being rushed down, so the set needs a comfortable defensive option while Savior is building.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer's notes on Savior, discussing when it is viable and the vulnerability of fast P2 Savior openings.

insight: Thorium Dynamo is strong even when green is bad, because its click ability provides gold. In higher-economy sets it is generally preferred over Drones because it costs less energy. Choosing how many Dynamos to get is similar to choosing how many Drones: aim for the right economy size for the build.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer's unit notes on Thorium Dynamo, discussing its role in economy-building.

insight: Tia Threnody opening lines are heavily set-dependent. As P1, DC DA is often played when threatening or getting early Tia is good. If blue is weak and a red sink exists, getting Blastforge in response to a naked Tia setup can be effective, threatening your own Tia next turn if they don't buy theirs.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer's unit notes on Tia Threnody, outlining set-dependent opening considerations.

insight: Gauss Fabricator: DD/CC/DD/Fab is often correct if you are confident it is a Fab set, the line is not punishable, and it is not a high-economy set. If fast attack is present in the set, this line leaves you very vulnerable for several turns, so a defensive plan is needed. With Xaetron in the set, heavy early green pairs well with the post-Fab floating.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer's unit notes on Gauss Fabricator openings and their interaction with pressure units and Xaetron.

insight: Colossus justifies a 3rd Engineer. In base+Colossus sets, Colossus wins easily against Tarsiers. It is not on the same level as Centurion or Defenseless Grid, and if there are counters like absorb denial or chill, you should think carefully before committing to DDE.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer's unit notes on Colossus and its impact on the standard Engineer-buying decision.

insight: Cryo Ray very often justifies more than one Conduit. Its strength comes from granularity freezes and freezing absorbers. When opponents mass Cryo Rays, the correct response is usually to also mass them while buying extra Engineers when possible and maintaining a backup absorber to avoid freezing exploits.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer's unit notes on Cryo Ray emphasizing its strategic depth around freeze exploitation.

insight: In Galvani Drone + Antima Comet sets, the key heuristic for whether to open 1. 11EE is whether you can sink the tech after the Comet fires and 'drone up' into a normal game even as the opponent clears your Galvanis. If you can't transition meaningfully after Galvanis are killed, 11EE is likely incorrect.
author: mqp
context: mqp offering the primary heuristic in the debate about whether opening 11EE is correct in Galvani+Antima sets.

insight: Chrono Filter is basically the only unit that justifies opening 1. 11EE (two Engineers turn 1) in Galvani Drone + Antima Comet sets. Without Chrono Filter, getting Engineers on turn 2 is more reasonable.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 distilling the Galvani+Antima opening debate to a single key condition for the aggressive 11EE line.

insight: In Chrono Filter + Galvani Drone + Antima Comet (base+), P2's fastest rush is likely a P2 autowin. P1's fastest rush in that set is bad. The Antima threat forces P1 to prepare defense while P2's Galvani-boosted economy becomes crushingly large.
author: mqp
context: mqp confirming prior testing that P2's fastest rush is an autowin in base+Chrono+Galvani+Antima.

insight: When both players get Antima Comet early and the dust settles, there is usually lots of soak and not much permanent attack — similar to Deadeye Operative games — and whoever has more economy wins. This makes large-econ Antima superior to rushed Antima in most cases.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer explaining why rushing Antima Comet tends to lead to a symmetric soak-heavy board state where economy wins.

insight: Wincer combined with Chrono Filter is significantly more 'autowin-esque' than Hannibull rush, because P1 cannot effectively out-rush P2's Chrono+Wincer and P2's mirror Wincer beats P1's rush. Good soak units (Shredder, Hannibull) are the primary counters to Chrono+Wincer.
author: extratricky
context: extratricky and leary93 debating the relative autowin strength of Hannibull rush vs Chrono+Wincer rush.

insight: 'Autowin' in Prismata specifically means a strategy where your opponent sees it coming and prepares optimally but still loses — not merely a strategy that wins when the opponent is blindsided or fails to adapt.
author: extratricky
context: extratricky clarifying the precise meaning of 'autowin' during a discussion about Hannibull and Wincer.

insight: In sets with Wincer, Wincer does not always need to fire; if the opponent walls up and lacks attack, the Wincer player can continue building economy and attack instead. However, if the Wincer player builds attack instead of Drones they likely cannot afford a second shot.
author: extratricky
context: extratricky and leary93 analyzing a specific Wincer position in analysis mode, discussing when firing vs holding is correct.

insight: Hannibull can function similarly to Thunderhead as a late-game defensive absorber, not just as an attacker. In sets with Galvani Drone, even a late Hannibull buy that only attacks once before dying may be worthwhile if it also provides soak.
author: leary93
context: leary93 arguing that Hannibull's value extends beyond pure attack in Galvani+Wincer set analysis.

insight: The four-unit combo of Chrono Filter + Galvani Drone + Hannibull + Wincer is likely a P2 autowin in base+. Units like Pixie, Immolite, Borehole Patroller, and Scorchilla that let P1 reach 7 attack more easily make the matchup more even. Good absorbers reduce the combo's dominance.
author: extratricky
context: extratricky and leary93 concluding a long analysis thread about the Chrono+Galvani+Hannibull+Wincer four-unit combo.

insight: Chrono Filter + Antima Comet is less broken than commonly perceived because the obvious fastest rush loses to defending, and both players tend to avoid their fastest rush, leading to high-economy games that approach 50/50. Galvani Drone alongside them amplifies this dynamic rather than making one rush dominant for P1.
author: extratricky
context: extratricky arguing that Chrono+Antima (and Chrono+Galvani+Antima) are more balanced than their reputation suggests, then partially walking it back after mqp's correction about P2 rush.

insight: Master Bot plays Rhinos poorly in sets where Electrovore rush is the correct strategy. In a set containing Electrovore, Master Bot bought Rhinos when the dominant line is considered to be a fast Electrovore opener.
author: nvlx
context: User uchinanchu shared a game vs Master Bot; nvlx (Fate) immediately noted the bot's Rhino purchases were wrong given an Electrovore-viable set.
